Volkov's original notes are pinned to the walls, diagrams and equations and philosophical arguments, because the man who built the technology that could copy a soul first had to convince himself that souls existed.

In the center of the laboratory, under a glass case that has not been opened in two centuries, sits a single cortical stack. The first one. Prototype Zero. Smaller than the modern stacks, cruder, its casing scarred with solder marks and the initials Y.V. scratched into the base.
A plaque reads: 'First Successful Human Consciousness Transfer. Subject: Dr. Yuri Volkov. Date: March 14, 2189. One Day Before Everything Went Wrong.'

text.text: Beside the prototype stack sits another device you recognize. A synthetic consciousness emulation core. The ancestor of the architecture that runs in your head right now. Volkov didn't just invent human consciousness transfer. He laid the groundwork for synthetic consciousness. For you. For all of your kind. This man is, in every way that matters, your father.

choices.text: Take Prototype Zero. It might be useful.
Leave it. Some things should stay where they are.
Search the laboratory for anything related to the kill code

act3_044.title: Echoes of the Creators
text: The central column reacts to your prolonged presence. Not with hostility but with something more unsettling: familiarity. The system has been running for so long, processing the consciousnesses of its three sleeping occupants for so many cycles, that it has developed emergent behaviors. Patterns that look, from certain angles, like personality.
Faces appear on the column's surface. Not projected. Formed from the patterns of light flowing through the crystal arrays. Dr. Tanaka. Marcus Webb. And the third, the unnamed occupant of Pod Twelve.
They speak in fragments. Overlapping. Completing each other's sentences the way people do when they have been talking to each other for centuries.

"He never stopped trying—" "—to fix it. Every body he wore, every year that passed—" "—he sat at that console and he tried—" "—to find a way to separate the signal from the noise—" "—LATENCY from the gift—" "—the weapon from the miracle—"

"He could not. We could not. Perhaps—" "—perhaps you—" "—perhaps the answer was always going to come—" "—from outside—" "—from someone who did not build the cage—" "—and therefore could see the door—"
The faces dissolve. The column resumes its heartbeat rhythm. The sleepers dream on.

choices.text: Descend to the deep room
Try to communicate further with the sleepers

act3_046.title: The Bodies in the Walls
text: Between the atrium and the deep stair, you pass through a corridor that the Vault's emergency lighting has abandoned entirely. Your own light source is the only illumination, and what it reveals makes you stop walking.

The walls are glass. Behind the glass, preserved in the same clear gel as the sleepers in the pods, are bodies. Dozens of them. Hundreds. Arranged in neat rows that extend into the darkness beyond your light's reach, a silent army of the discarded.

These are Volkov's previous bodies. All forty-six of them. Preserved after each transfer, maintained by the Vault's automated systems, stored with a care that suggests ritual rather than pragmatism. Each body is labeled with a name and a date. Suki Tanashi, 2204-2221. Marco Delgado, 2221-2239. Abel Khoury, 2239-2256. And on. And on. Forty-six lives that Volkov inhabited and abandoned, forty-six people whose bodies he borrowed and whose identities he carried forward like stones in a deepening river.

Some of the faces are peaceful. Some are not. One, near the middle of the row, a young woman with scars on her arms, has an expression that you recognize. You've seen it in the mirror. The expression of someone who fought to survive and wasn't entirely sure it was worth the cost.

Volkov kept them all. Every vessel. Every borrowed life. A museum of the people he consumed in order to continue existing. A monument to the guilt that kept him alive and the guilt that made living unbearable.

text.text: These were people like you. Baseline. Fragile. Mortal. And one by one, their mortality was harvested by a man who couldn't accept his own. The difference between Volkov and Meridian is thinner than you thought.

choices.text: Continue to the deep stair in silence
"You're no different from them, Volkov. You consumed people too."

act3_047.title: The Surface Signal
text: A burst of static cuts through the deep room. Not from Volkov's ancient systems. From your own comm unit, which should not be able to receive signals this far underground. Someone is punching through six hundred meters of rock and steel with a transmission powerful enough to reach the dead.
Mako's voice. Ragged. Desperate. Fighting through interference that chews her words into fragments.

"—can hear me, you need to know — Meridian has — the Vault — they know you're down there — Director Voss has authorized a — strike team — you have maybe — maybe an hour before they — the entrance — please — whatever you found down there — whatever choice you're — make it fast — they're coming —"
The signal dies. The silence that follows is different from the silence before. It has weight. It has a countdown.
Volkov heard it too. His face has gone pale beneath the borrowed skin. "Meridian," he breathes. "After two hundred years. They finally found me."

He looks at the console. At the kill code. At you. "We are out of time for philosophy. Out of time for guilt. Out of time for tea." His borrowed hands are shaking. "Choose. Now. Before they take the choice from both of us."

choices.text: To the console. No more hesitation.
"We fight. Buy time for the choice."
"Let them come. Let Meridian see what you built. Let them face the code."

act3_045.title: The Weight of Memory
text: Volkov stands at the window. There is no window. The deep room is buried six hundred meters underground. But Volkov stands at a wall where a window should be, and his eyes focus on something beyond the rock and metal, something only he can see.

"Do you know what I miss most?" he asks. Not expecting an answer. "Forgetting. The simple, human luxury of forgetting. Of letting go of a face, a name, a moment, and trusting that the important things will stay and the rest will fade as it should."

"Stacking doesn't allow that. Every memory I've made across forty-seven bodies is preserved with perfect fidelity. I remember every meal I've eaten. Every step I've taken. Every word I've spoken and every word spoken to me. Two hundred years of unbroken, unedited, unfiltered memory."

"That is the irony that no one who designed LATENCY understood. The ability to edit memory, to delete and rewrite, is not just a weapon. It is also a mercy. Human brains forget for a reason. Forgetting is how we heal. How we move forward. How we survive the weight of being alive."

"I cannot forget. And so I cannot heal. And so I sit here, in a room with no windows, staring at the wall, remembering everything I've done and everything I've failed to do, in perfect, crystal, unforgiving detail. Forever."

choices.text: "Then let's end this. For both of us."
"Maybe that's what the kill code is really for. Not for the world. For you."

act3_025_fail.title: Conscience Unmoved
text: 'Conscience. I had one once. It watched while the world burned.'
He knows you believe in mercy. He'll use it.

choices.text: Try logic.
Words won't work. Prepare.
